messages.content:
A customer hired you to assist with their design of migrating their application to the cloud. Their on-premises server has two internal SSD drives where data is ingested and processed. After 14 days, as data gets colder and rarely touched, the application moves the cold data from SSDs to a volume backed by HDD drives, in case the data is ever requested again.

How would you design the most cost-effective Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volume storage for the application of an Amazon EC2 Instance that maintains the same or better durability of the data?


Use gp3 volume type for the ingest and processing. Use sc1 volume type for the long term storage.

Use st1 volume type for ingest and processing. Use sc1 volume type for long term storage.

Use Ephemeral volume for the ingest and processing. Use sc1 volume type for the long term storage.

Use io2 volume type for the ingest and processing. Use st1 volume type for the long term storage.

The most cost-effective design for the Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volume storage would be to use gp3 volume type for the ingest and processing and sc1 volume type for long-term storage.

The gp3 volume type is suitable for the ingest and processing phase as it provides a balance of cost, performance, and durability. It offers lower cost per GB and provides the option to adjust performance based on IOPS and throughput requirements. This ensures efficient data ingestion and processing while keeping costs low.

For long-term storage, the sc1 volume type is ideal. It is designed for frequently accessed, throughput-intensive workloads and provides the lowest cost per GB of all EBS volume types. Since the data is rarely accessed after being moved from SSDs, using sc1 volumes help to further optimize costs without compromising on durability.

Therefore, using gp3 volume type for ingest and processing and sc1 volume type for long-term storage would provide the most cost-effective and durable solution for migrating the application to the cloud.

You have a gp2 volume that never reaches its provisioned IOPS/Throughput baseline in CloudWatch. It has not exceeded burst credits, yet the application owner reports that the application frequently is slow to respond, with some actions taking several seconds to run. You think the application may have I/O spikes that are too short to be visible in CloudWatch.

What CloudWatch metric would you monitor in order to confirm that theory?


VolumeConsumedReadWriteOps

VolumeTotalReadTime

VolumeQueueLength

VolumeIdleTime

To confirm the theory that the application may have I/O spikes that are too short to be visible in CloudWatch, you would monitor the "VolumeQueueLength" CloudWatch metric.

The VolumeQueueLength metric measures the length of the volume queue, which is essentially the number of pending I/O requests in the queue. If the application frequently experiences I/O spikes that are too short to be captured by CloudWatch, the VolumeQueueLength metric will provide insight into the queue length during those spikes.

If the volume has short I/O spikes that exceed the provisioned IOPS baseline, the queue length will increase, indicating that there are pending I/O requests waiting to be processed. This indicates that the application is experiencing latency due to these short I/O spikes.

So, monitoring the VolumeQueueLength metric will help confirm whether the application's slow response times and delayed actions are caused by short I/O spikes that are not visible in CloudWatch.

You have an application that does sequential video transcoding and requires 350 MiB/s of throughput.

What Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volume type provides the best option for this workload?


sc1

gp2

gp3

st1

fast answer

The best option for this workload would be the "st1" volume type.

The st1 volume type is designed for throughput-intensive workloads, such as sequential video transcoding. It offers the highest throughput among all the Amazon EBS volume types, making it an ideal choice for applications that require a sustained high level of throughput.

In this case, where the application requires 350 MiB/s of throughput, the st1 volume type would provide the best performance to meet this requirement.

An application requires block storage that can deliver 100,000 IOPS sustained for several hours a day, with sub-millisecond latency. The data is considered mission-critical and the customer is looking for highly fault-tolerant infrastructure.

Which block storage type would you recommend?


instance store volumes, with an EC2 instance type that can deliver the IOPS.

Multiple gp3 volumes with host-based software striping, and an EC2 instance that can deliver the IOPS.

io2 Block Express, with an EC2 instance type that can deliver the IOPS.

io2, with an EC2 instance type that can deliver the IOPS.

Fast answer

I would recommend using "io2 Block Express" with an EC2 instance type that can deliver the required IOPS.

The io2 Block Express volume type is designed for high-performance, mission-critical workloads that require sustained IOPS and low latency. It offers the highest levels of performance and durability among all the EBS volume types.

To achieve the desired 100,000 IOPS sustained for several hours a day, io2 Block Express volumes would be the most suitable option. They provide consistent performance, low latency, and high durability, making them ideal for mission-critical applications.

Pairing the io2 Block Express volumes with an EC2 instance type that can deliver the required IOPS will ensure that the application's performance needs are met while maintaining fault-tolerant infrastructure.

Therefore, I recommend using io2 Block Express with an EC2 instance type that can deliver the required IOPS for this workload.

You need to share an Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) snapshot from AWS Account 1 to Account 2.

What configuration would you recommend?


Allow EBS snapshot create access to Account 2.

Give Account 2 full control of Account 1.

Turn off encryption on Account 1 before taking the snapshot.

Modify the snapshot permissions to allow access to Account 2.

fast answer

To share an Amazon EBS snapshot from Account 1 to Account 2, I would recommend modifying the snapshot permissions to allow access to Account 2.

By modifying the snapshot permissions, you can specifically grant access to Account 2 to view and use the snapshot. This ensures that Account 2 can access and utilize the snapshot as needed while maintaining control over who can access it.

Therefore, the recommended configuration would be to modify the snapshot permissions to allow access to Account 2.

Which is a best practice for Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) cost optimization?


Use host-based RAID-0 to deliver the total required capacity for the application

Detach volumes from EC2 instances when not in active use

Minimize the number of volume resize operations required over time

Use newer volume types when appropriate

fast answer

A best practice for Amazon EBS cost optimization is to minimize the number of volume resize operations required over time.

Resizing EBS volumes can incur additional costs as it involves modifying the capacity of the volume. To optimize costs, it is recommended to carefully plan and allocate the appropriate volume sizes when initially provisioning them for your application. Consider the anticipated storage needs and growth requirements to avoid frequent volume resizing.

By accurately estimating the required size for your EBS volumes and provisioning them accordingly, you can minimize the need for frequent resizing operations, which helps to optimize costs.

Therefore, minimizing the number of volume resize operations required over time is a best practice for Amazon EBS cost optimization.

Your organization's security policies mandate that all new Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes are encrypted.

What is the most efficient and cost-effective way to achieve this?


Create an AWS IAM Policy with a Deny action for the ec2:RunInstances action if the instance's Amazon EBS volumes are not encrypted. Apply this policy to all users.

In the Amazon EC2 Management Console, browse to the EC2 Dashboard. Under Account Attributes, choose Settings. Under EBS Storage, choose Always encrypt new Amazon EBS volumes. Do this for all Regions.

Use AWS Config to detect instances which are launched with unencrypted Amazon EBS volumes. Automatically invoke an AWS Systems Manager Automation playbook to remediate this. Set this up in all Regions.

Subscribe to your preferred full-disk encryption software via the AWS Marketplace. Use Amazon EC2 UserData to ensure all instances launch this software at boot time.

ffast answer

The most efficient and cost-effective way to ensure that all new Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes are encrypted is to use the following method:

In the Amazon EC2 Management Console, browse to the EC2 Dashboard. Under Account Attributes, choose Settings. Under EBS Storage, choose "Always encrypt new Amazon EBS volumes." Do this for all Regions.

This method allows you to enable the default encryption setting for all new EBS volumes in your AWS account. By enabling this setting, any new EBS volumes that are created within your account will automatically be encrypted, ensuring compliance with your organization's security policies.

Enabling this setting at the account level ensures that encryption will be applied consistently across all regions and for all users in your AWS account. The automatic encryption of new EBS volumes eliminates the need to manually enable encryption for each individual volume, saving time and effort.

Therefore, using the option to "Always encrypt new Amazon EBS volumes" in the EC2 Dashboard settings is the most efficient and cost-effective way to ensure encryption for all new EBS volumes.

You have a gp3 volume in production and are unsure whether your application workload is more sequential or more random. You examine the CloudWatch monitoring graphs in Amazon EC2 console for the individual Amazon EBS volume. You notice high bandwidth reported (KiB/s) with only a few Ops/s being reported.

What does this tell you?


Your application is being throttled and you need to provision more IOPS

Your workload is more sequential than random.

You have a mixed random and sequential application workload

Your workload is more random than sequential

fast answer

The observed high bandwidth reported (KiB/s) with only a few Ops/s being reported indicates that your workload is more sequential than random.

In this scenario, the high bandwidth, which is the data transfer rate in KiB/s, indicates that the workload is primarily focused on reading or writing large sequential blocks of data. Sequential workloads involve continuous or sequential accesses to data rather than random access.

On the other hand, the low Ops/s (operations per second) count suggests that there are few random read or write operations occurring. Random workloads involve accessing data in a non-sequential or random order, typically involving smaller block sizes.

Therefore, based on the high bandwidth and low Ops/s count, it can be inferred that your workload is more sequential than random.

Which  Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) data can be captured using AWS CloudTrail? (Select TWO)

Number of Amazon EBS volumes with more than 10,000 IOPs sustained for at least one minute within the last 24 hours.List of Amazon EBS volumes with more than 90% disk capacity utilization.List of io2 volumes that have experienced peak IOPs = provisioned IOPs within the last 15 days.Number of volumes created by an AWS IAM user within the last 24 hours.List of AWS IAM roles that have been used to delete Amazon EBS snapshots in an account.

fast answer

The two Amazon EBS data that can be captured using AWS CloudTrail are:

1. Number of volumes created by an AWS IAM user within the last 24 hours.
2. List of AWS IAM roles that have been used to delete Amazon EBS snapshots in an account.

AWS CloudTrail captures API-level activity and can provide information about actions taken on Amazon EBS volumes, snapshots, and related resources. In the given options, capturing the number of volumes created by an IAM user and tracking IAM roles used to delete EBS snapshots are the capabilities provided by CloudTrail.

It is important to note that the other options listed do not specifically align with the functionality of AWS CloudTrail, which is focused on auditing and recording API activity rather than providing real-time metrics or detailed performance data.

Your management requires you to implement accidental deletion protection for snapshots of production volumes.

What method can you NOT use to accomplish this?


Create an IAM policy to deny SnapshotDelete permissions for all users.

Use AWS Backup for all production snapshots and only allow users read access to the backup vault.

Enable WORM on all production snapshots.

Enable EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin and set a retention policy for production volumes.

fast answer

The method you cannot use to implement accidental deletion protection for snapshots of production volumes is:

Enable EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin and set a retention policy for production volumes.

The EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin is a feature that provides a safety net for EBS snapshots by retaining deleted snapshots for a specific period. However, it does not protect against accidental deletion of snapshots when the Recycle Bin is not enabled.

To implement accidental deletion protection for snapshots of production volumes, you should consider one of the other options:

1. Create an IAM policy to deny SnapshotDelete permissions for all users: This restricts the ability to delete snapshots and helps prevent accidental deletions.

2. Use AWS Backup for all production snapshots and only allow users read access to the backup vault: AWS Backup is a fully managed backup service that simplifies data protection. By providing read access only to the backup vault, it helps prevent accidental deletions.

3. Enable WORM (Write Once Read Many) on all production snapshots: This enforces retention and prevents the modification or deletion of snapshots once they are written, ensuring data immutability.

These methods provide different approaches to protect against accidental deletion of production snapshots, but enabling EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin is not a valid option for this purpose.

Your management requires you to implement accidental deletion protection for snapshots of production volumes.

What method can you NOT use to accomplish this?


Create an IAM policy to deny SnapshotDelete permissions for all users.

Use AWS Backup for all production snapshots and only allow users read access to the backup vault.

Enable WORM on all production snapshots.

Enable EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin and set a retention policy for production volumes.

what the right answer fast answer please

The method you cannot use to implement accidental deletion protection for snapshots of production volumes is:

Enable EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin and set a retention policy for production volumes.

The EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin is not a feature available in AWS. Therefore, you cannot use it to accomplish accidental deletion protection for snapshots of production volumes. This option is incorrect.

The correct options to implement accidental deletion protection for snapshots of production volumes are:

1. Create an IAM policy to deny SnapshotDelete permissions for all users: This policy will restrict the ability to delete snapshots and help prevent accidental deletions.

2. Use AWS Backup for all production snapshots and only allow users read access to the backup vault: AWS Backup is a fully managed backup service that simplifies data protection. By providing read access only to the backup vault, it helps prevent accidental deletions.

3. Enable WORM (Write Once Read Many) on all production snapshots: Enabling WORM enforces retention and prevents the modification or deletion of snapshots once they are written, ensuring data immutability.

Please note that there is no EBS Snapshots Recycle Bin feature available in AWS, and therefore it cannot be used to implement accidental deletion protection for snapshots.
